surface.name: Hosted Supabase project queried from React
surface.selection_basis: Workshop selection policy (2026-07-19): when official docs present peer routes, commit to the fastest and most commonly used documented route. Prefer the vendor's first-listed or recommended quickstart, dashboard/console over CLI when both are peers, and the route with an explicit vendor time claim or fewest documented steps when tied. Selected route: Hosted Supabase project queried from React. The official React framework quickstart explicitly teaches project creation, sample database setup, client configuration, a query, and a rendered instrument list. React is a representative complete candidate, not a platform-wide default over the other framework, local, AI, API-key, demo, and use-case routes. Prior surface before policy commit: Supabase Getting Started router.

alternatives_considered.name: Framework quickstarts
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The page offers many framework-specific paths with different language, package, environment, and runtime requirements.

alternatives_considered.name: Local Development and CLI quickstart
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: This is a local stack route that requires a CLI and Docker-compatible runtime, distinct from the hosted framework quickstarts.

alternatives_considered.name: Build with AI tools
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: This is a workflow router for Agent Plugin, MCP server, Agent Skills, and prompts, not one application milestone.

alternatives_considered.name: React quickstart
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It has a concrete queried-data terminal state but is only one of many co-equal framework quickstarts.

entry_point.developer_intent: Start building with Supabase after selecting the platform but before selecting a workflow, use case, framework, or application type.
assumed_prior_state: Discovery is complete and the developer has selected Supabase.
No framework, local-versus-hosted approach, use case, or target application has been selected.

documented_first_success.official_milestone: The Supabase React quickstart ends when the browser renders the instrument list (violin, viola, cello) queried from the Supabase database.
documented_first_success.normalized_outcome: The browser displays violin, viola, and cello from the Supabase database.
documented_first_success.observable_completion_signal: The browser displays violin, viola, and cello from the Supabase database.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: The browser displays violin, viola, and cello from the Supabase database.

documented_first_success.why_this_is_the_boundary: Selected under workshop fastest/most-used policy; terminal taken from the committed official candidate route.

prerequisites.requirement: Select a workflow, use case, framework quickstart, web app demo, or mobile tutorial from the official Getting Started router.

Hosted framework quickstarts require a Supabase project created from the Dashboard in an organization, or programmatically through the Management API or MCP server. Project creation includes waiting for the new database to launch before continuing to SQL setup.

The Local Development route requires the Supabase CLI and a Docker-compatible container runtime; supported choices listed by Supabase are Docker Desktop, Rancher Desktop, Podman, and OrbStack on macOS.
Framework and mobile routes require the language, framework, package manager, and local runtime used by the selected guide.

primary_path.action: Open the Supabase Getting Started page.
details: The top level presents Build with AI tools, API Keys, and Local Development.
The page then presents Use cases, Framework quickstarts, Web app demos, and Mobile tutorials.
primary_path.success_signal: The documented route groups and choices are visible.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: No wait is documented at the router.

primary_path.action: Choose the route whose documented outcome should define first success.
details: Workflow choices are Build with AI tools, API Keys, and Local Development.
Use-case choices are AI, Vectors, and embeddings; Subscription Payments; and Partner Gallery.

Main-content framework quickstarts are React, Next.js, Nuxt, Hono, RedwoodJS, Flutter, iOS SwiftUI, Android Kotlin, SvelteKit, SolidJS, Vue, TanStack Start, and Refine; the documentation navigation also exposes additional framework guides.
Web app demos are Next.js, React, Vue 3, Nuxt 3, Angular, RedwoodJS, Svelte, SvelteKit, and Refine.
Mobile tutorials are Flutter, Expo React Native, Expo React Native Social Auth, Android Kotlin, iOS Swift, Ionic React, Ionic Vue, and Ionic Angular.
primary_path.input: Desired development workflow and application target
primary_path.success_signal: A human has selected one official route.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Supabase does not select a universal route on the general page.

primary_path.action: Sign in to the Supabase Dashboard and create a new Supabase project from an organization the developer belongs to.
details: The Management API and MCP server are documented alternative project-creation methods; Dashboard is selected here.
primary_path.success_signal: The project is created and begins launching its database.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Organization permissions, Free Plan active-project limits, or project-creation validation can block creation.

primary_path.action: Wait until the new Supabase project and database are up and running.
primary_path.success_signal: The project Dashboard and SQL Editor are available.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: The quickstart requires the project to be up and running before database setup; no exact launch duration is stated.

primary_path.action: Open the project's SQL Editor, paste the supplied instruments SQL, and select Run.
details: The SQL creates public.instruments with generated bigint id and non-null name, inserts violin, viola, and cello, grants select to anon, enables row level security, and creates a public read policy for anon.
A prefilled SQL Editor link is offered as an alternative to manual copying.
primary_path.success_signal: The SQL Editor reports successful execution and the table contains the three sample rows.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: SQL errors, an existing conflicting table or policy, or insufficient project permission must be corrected before the app query can succeed.

primary_path.action: If Data API was disabled during project setup, open Integrations > Data API, enable it, and expose the instruments table.
details: Automatically expose new tables is an optional project setting.
primary_path.success_signal: The instruments table is exposed to the Data API.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: This step is not needed when the Data API and public-table exposure are already enabled; without exposure the client query cannot return the table.

primary_path.action: Run npm create vite@latest my-app -- --template react.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Node, npm, network, package, or filesystem errors block scaffolding.

details: Installing Supabase Agent Skills is a separately labeled optional step and is skipped for the minimal candidate.
primary_path.output: React app with Supabase client library
primary_path.failure_or_wait: npm, network, lockfile, or permission errors must be corrected before code can import the client.

primary_path.action: Open the project's Connect panel and copy the Project URL and publishable key.
details: The publishable key is the documented public-client credential for this route.
primary_path.success_signal: Both values are available for the Vite environment file.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Using a secret key in a public React client is not the documented route and would expose a privileged credential.

primary_path.action: Create .env.local in my-app and set VITE_SUPABASE_URL to the Project URL and VITE_SUPABASE_PUBLISHABLE_KEY to the publishable key.
primary_path.success_signal: Both documented Vite variables are present with the project's values.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Missing, mistyped, or wrong-project values prevent client creation or query the wrong project.

primary_path.action: Replace src/App.jsx with the quickstart code that creates the Supabase client, selects all rows from instruments in getInstruments, stores the returned data, handles errors, and renders each instrument name in a list.
primary_path.input: Configured environment and official App.jsx example
primary_path.success_signal: App.jsx imports createClient, calls supabase.from('instruments').select(), and maps the result to list items.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Syntax, environment, table, policy, or Data API errors are logged by the example and must be corrected before rows render.

primary_path.action: Run npm run dev from my-app.
primary_path.success_signal: Vite serves the app at http://localhost:5173.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Port, dependency, build, or environment errors must be corrected and the server restarted.

primary_path.action: Open http://localhost:5173 and verify the instrument list.
primary_path.input: Running React app and readable instruments table
primary_path.success_signal: The browser displays violin, viola, and cello from the Supabase database.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: If the list is absent, inspect browser and terminal errors and verify the Project URL, publishable key, Data API exposure, table, grant, RLS policy, and running dev server.

branches.path: Follow the selected framework's hosted-project journey. The common description is to create a Supabase project, add sample database data, and query it from an application, but framework tools and some security or migration steps differ. The React example creates a new project in an organization, waits for the new database to launch, opens SQL Editor, runs the supplied SQL to create and secure an instruments table, creates a Vite app, optionally installs Agent Skills, installs supabase-js, adds the project URL and publishable key, writes the query UI, starts the app, and verifies the instrument list in a browser.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Produces a queried-data application result with framework-specific prerequisites and commands.

branches.path: Choose npm, yarn, pnpm, or Homebrew and use that choice consistently; install the Supabase CLI; initialize the project in a repository; start the local stack; then open the local Supabase instance at http://localhost:54323. On an untrusted public network, create a separate Docker network bound to 127.0.0.1 and start Supabase with that network ID instead of exposing the stack publicly.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Removes the hosted-project requirement but adds CLI, container-runtime, installation-method, startup, network-safety, and local-service gates.

branches.condition: Build with AI tools is selected.
branches.path: Choose among the Agent Plugin, MCP server, Agent Skills, or ready-to-use prompts for the development workflow. A separate branch for building AI into an application routes to deploying MCP servers or using vectors and embeddings.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Requires another tool or product choice and does not establish one executed application boundary on the overview page.

branches.path: Open the project's Connect dialog for the usual key, or Settings > API Keys for a specific one. Choose a publishable key for a public client component or a secret key for a protected backend; legacy anon and service_role keys remain separately available. Creating or copying a key is credential setup, and this page does not execute a universal application request.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Produces a credential-handling path with public-versus-secret security decisions rather than an application result.

branches.condition: A web app demo or mobile tutorial is selected.
branches.path: Choose the listed web or mobile framework and follow its larger application tutorial. Most web and mobile descriptions build user-management functionality across Supabase Database, Auth, and Storage; Android builds product management, and Expo Social Auth focuses on social authentication.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Extends the journey beyond the smaller sample-query quickstarts and changes framework, device, authentication, and storage steps.

branches.path: Choose AI, Vectors, and embeddings; the external Subscription Payments starter; or the external Partner Gallery. Each route has a different repository, platform feature set, and terminal result.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Requires a use-case-specific guide or repository before an atomic path can be established.

branches.condition: The hosted project is created under the Free Plan.
branches.path: Use an organization whose single subscription is the Free Plan. Supabase allows two active free projects and does not count paused projects against the quota. Within an organization, the limit is calculated across all members who are Owners or Admins, so an Owner or Admin who has exhausted the quota blocks another active free project in that organization. Create another Free Plan organization or change the affected member's role if necessary. Do not mix paid and free projects in one organization because billing and plan selection are organization-scoped.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Can block project creation before provisioning and makes organization membership and plan placement part of the hosted first mile.

branches.condition: A Free Plan project later has low activity over a seven-day period.
branches.path: Supabase may classify the project as inactive and sends the project owner a warning email roughly one week before pausing, followed by a confirmation email if the project is paused. Visiting the project in the Dashboard or generating sufficient API or connected-application database activity after the warning can prevent the pause. A paused project can be resumed from its organization and project page for up to 90 days; paid-plan projects are not automatically paused for inactivity.
branches.effect_on_first_success: This is a post-creation retention gate, not a universal signup requirement, but it can remove an earlier hosted success if the free project remains low activity.

friction_gates.description: The general Getting Started page requires a route, framework, and application-outcome choice that changes the entire first-mile path.
Hosted quickstarts require a Supabase project in an organization and a provisioning wait for the new database to launch; the local CLI route does not require that same hosted-project starting state.

The Free Plan allows two active projects, with the organization quota affected by every Owner or Admin member. Each organization has one plan, so free and paid projects cannot be mixed in the same organization.
A hosted quickstart waits for database provisioning. Separately, a low-activity Free Plan project can receive a warning email and be paused after the documented seven-day activity window.
Local and framework routes add different CLI, container, language, framework, and package installation requirements.
Hosted client applications need the project URL and an appropriate API key; public and secret keys have materially different placement and security rules.

Can I mix free and paid projects in a single organization?

uncertainties.question: Which official Supabase route should define the canonical workshop journey?
uncertainties.impact: The answer changes whether the first mile is a local stack, a hosted queried-data app, an AI-assisted workflow, a credential setup, or a larger web or mobile tutorial.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The general Getting Started page presents all route groups without naming one universal default.

excluded_after_success: Executing any route-specific setup until a human selects the canonical Supabase route
For a framework sample-query route, later Auth, additional inserts, Storage, and drop-in UI components
For local development, later schema, migration, deployment, and hosted-project workflows
